title: XTab: Cross-table Pretraining for Tabular Transformers
authors: Bingzhao Zhu (EPFL / Cornell, intern at AWS), Xingjian Shi (Boson AI), Nick Erickson (AWS), Mu Li (Boson AI), George Karypis (AWS), Mahsa Shoaran (EPFL)
tokenization_method: Per-column FT-Transformer-style featurizer kept DATA-SPECIFIC, plus a CROSS-TABLE-SHARED transformer backbone. Each row -> token sequence E in R^{c x d} (c columns, d=192) by a tokenizer that mirrors FT-Transformer (Gorishniy 2021): numerical value x_k -> x_k * W_k + b_k with a per-column trainable vector W_k in R^d and bias b_k; categorical value -> lookup into a per-dataset embedding matrix in R^{Ncat x d}. A learned [CLS] token is appended for prediction/contrastive pooling. The decisive architectural move: the tokenizer (featurizer) and the projection heads are NOT shared across tables and are RANDOMLY RE-INITIALIZED + trained from scratch on every new downstream table; ONLY the transformer blocks (the inter-feature interaction engine) are pretrained and transferred. So XTab transfers a column-agnostic feature-INTERACTION prior, not a tokenizer or a vocabulary. The authors explicitly state XTab 'does not attempt to learn a universal tokenizer for all tables' — it learns a generalizable weight initialization for the shared backbone instead.
feature_types_handled: numerical + categorical only (text cells coerced to categorical). Missing values handled at preprocessing: numerical NaNs imputed with column mean, categorical NaNs treated as an extra category. No native temporal/time-varying axis (each row is a static unordered token set). No free-text/string semantics (unlike CARTE/TabLLM) — text is just another category id.
token_granularity: per-feature (one token = one column), identical to FT-Transformer. Sequence length = number of columns + 1 ([CLS]). Variable column count across tables is the whole point: the shared transformer ingests variable-length sequences, which is why an MLP backbone could not be shared but a transformer can.
numerical_strategy: raw scalar passed through a per-column affine map (x_k * W_k + b_k), i.e. the plain FT-Transformer linear numerical tokenizer. NO piecewise-linear / periodic / quantile-bin embedding (no PLR, no Gorishniy-2022 numerical embeddings). Numerical features are z-score normalized (mean/std from train only); regression targets are also standardized.
categorical_strategy: per-dataset learned embedding lookup table in R^{Ncat x d}, integer-indexed. No hashing, no target/CatBoost encoding, no LM embedding of category strings. The lookup table is data-specific and re-learned per downstream table.
high_cardinality_handling: none specific. High-cardinality categoricals inflate the per-dataset lookup matrix (Ncat x d) and are re-learned from scratch per table; unseen categories at test time get no meaningful vector (no sub-word / LM fallback). 20 of 104 AMLB datasets were DROPPED from the head-to-head because they would not fit in 16 GB GPU memory — large category vocabularies / many columns are an implicit failure mode here.
temporal_handling: none. No patching, recurrence, or channel-as-token; no time axis. Time-varying features would have to be hand-flattened into static columns before tokenization.
scales_to_many_features: Better than sample-count-bound models (TabPFN) because inference cost is independent of training-set size, so the authors claim 'XTab also works for large tables'. BUT per-column tokens give O(c^2) attention in column count for FT-Transformer/Saint-v backbones (Fastformer offered as the linear-attention escape hatch for many columns). Pretraining cost grows LINEARLY with the number of pretraining tables, which is exactly why federated learning (FedAvg) is introduced to distribute it across a T4 cluster (~30k GPU-hours total). Crucially, 20/104 AMLB datasets were excluded for not fitting in 16 GB GPU memory — a concrete wide-table / high-cardinality memory ceiling. No experiment isolates the 70+-column regime.
downstream_model: Tabular transformer. Three interchangeable shared backbones tested: FT-Transformer (default; 3 blocks, d=192, 8 heads, ~<1M params), Fastformer (additive attention, linear in sequence length, for many-column efficiency), and Saint-v (a variable-column variant of SAINT with both column-wise and row-wise attention). All three benefit from pretraining (>50% win rate); FT-Transformer is the headline backbone.
pretraining_objective: Self-supervised cross-table pretraining on 52 of 104 AMLB tables (split alphabetically), with three objective options, all run through DATA-SPECIFIC projection heads: (1) RECONSTRUCTION — recover x from a corrupted view x_tilde (60% of entries resampled from each column's empirical marginal, SCARF-style), CE loss on categorical + MSE on numerical columns; (2) CONTRASTIVE — InfoNCE/SimCLR with (x, x_tilde) positives and other batch rows negatives; (3) SUPERVISED 'pre-finetuning' — predict each table's own label column. Reconstruction is empirically best (71.0% win rate light finetuning, 56.1% heavy at 2000 steps). Backbone trained by FEDERATED AVERAGING (FedAvg): one client per table holds its own featurizer + head + a copy of the shared backbone; server averages only the shared-backbone deltas every N local steps (N=5 default; larger N = cheaper comms but worse downstream).
key_results: On 84 AMLB tasks (regression + binary + multiclass; 2-fold cross-pretrain), XTab beats its no-pretrain FT-Transformer counterpart in EVERY finetuning regime (default-HPO mean rank, lower=better): XTab-l 9.21 vs FTT-l 10.19; XTab-h 6.93 vs FTT-h 7.29; XTab-best 4.39 vs FTT-best 4.94. XTab-best is the best DEEP model and 2nd overall, but CatBoost is best overall (rank 2.98 default / 3.25 HPO) — XTab does NOT beat GBDTs. Ablations: reconstruction > contrastive > supervised; more pretraining tasks (1 -> 18 -> 52) monotonically helps; only the transformer BLOCKS (not [CLS], not featurizers/heads) carry transferable knowledge; the pretraining advantage is LARGEST under tight finetuning budgets (light finetune, fewer epochs) and shrinks with heavy finetuning due to catastrophic forgetting in the small (<1M-param) backbone.
baselines_compared: Its own FT-Transformer (FTT) without pretraining (l/h/best variants) as the direct counterpart; TransTab (sl + cl, classification only) as the prior cross-table-transfer transformer; tree models RandomForest, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost (AutoGluon defaults); neural baselines AutoGluon NN and FastAI tabular. Both default-hyperparameter and HPO (100 trials / 1h) settings.
transferable_idea: Decouple the per-table TOKENIZER from the cross-table INTERACTION ENGINE: keep a data-specific featurizer (so any column count/type/vocabulary is admissible) but pretrain and reuse ONLY the column-agnostic transformer blocks as a portable feature-interaction prior, fused across many tables via federated averaging. For a 70+-heterogeneous-feature pipeline this says: you can warm-start the attention/interaction stack from a large corpus of unrelated tables without ever aligning schemas or sharing a vocabulary — the tokenizer stays bespoke per dataset and only the relational reasoning transfers.
limitations: Authors-stated: (1) XTab still LOSES to CatBoost — pretraining narrows but does not close the deep-vs-GBDT gap. (2) Catastrophic forgetting in the small backbone erodes the pretraining benefit under heavy finetuning. (3) NOT compatible with zero-shot / few-shot: featurizers and heads are re-trained from scratch, so 'sufficient finetuning data is still needed' and the subsampled-data experiment shows the advantage does NOT grow as finetuning data shrinks. (4) Restricted to numerical+categorical; text is coerced to categorical, no temporal support. (5) 20/104 datasets dropped for 16 GB GPU memory limits. Future work: bridge the GBDT gap, add language/vision multimodal foundation pieces.
pitfalls: 1) DOES NOT transfer the tokenizer — the headline 'cross-table pretraining' transfers only the interaction backbone; the numerical/categorical featurizer is randomly re-initialized per table. Anyone hoping XTab gives a reusable heterogeneous-feature TOKENIZER (the topic's actual question) will be disappointed; CARTE/TabPFN-v2 are the right comparisons for transferable tokenization. 2) Numerical encoding is the WEAK plain-affine FT-Transformer tokenizer — no PLR/periodic embeddings — so it carries the FT-Transformer numerical-handling weaknesses this topic's numerical-embeddings route tries to fix. 3) Gains are real but MODEST and budget-dependent: largest under light finetuning, shrinking to ~rank 6.93 vs 7.29 under heavy finetuning, and still below GBDTs. 4) Federated learning here is NOT for privacy — it is purely an engineering trick to parallelize linearly-growing pretraining cost across a GPU cluster; FedAvg with unitary scalarization is a plain distributed-SGD with periodic backbone averaging. 5) No high-cardinality, no large-column (70+) isolated study, and a hard 16 GB memory wall (20 datasets excluded). 6) Backbone is <1M params — far smaller than 'foundation model' connotes; the prior is shallow.
relevance_to_topic: A reference point for whether a SHARED, pretrained tabular transformer backbone can be warm-started across heterogeneous tables — answer: yes for the interaction layers, no for the tokenizer, and only to GBDT-trailing performance — so it informs the learning-signal layer (transfer pretraining) more than the token-granularity layer, and warns that cross-table transfer in 2023 left the heterogeneous-feature tokenizer firmly data-specific.
code_available: yes — https://github.com/BingzhaoZhu/XTab (code + sample pretrained checkpoints; built on AutoGluon; AMLB at openml.github.io/automlbenchmark).
